findings.description: offset = page * page_size with a documented 1-based page skips the entire first page (page 1 yields offset page_size), so the first page_size users are unreachable; should be (page - 1) * page_size.
User-supplied name_pattern is compiled and run as a raw regex over every row, enabling catastrophic-backtracking ReDoS denial-of-service via a crafted pattern.
re.compile on attacker-controlled name_pattern has no try/except, so a malformed regex raises an uncaught re.error and 500s the request.

Query has no SQL LIMIT/OFFSET; it fetchall()s the entire (optionally department-filtered) user table into memory and filters/sorts/paginates in Python, so pagination does nothing for the DB and memory blows up at scale.
display_name is assumed to be a non-null string; a NULL value yields None, crashing matcher.search(None) (TypeError) on line 60 and None.lower() (AttributeError) on line 62.
page_size is clamped only on the upper bound; page_size <= 0 yields a perpetually empty window and negative values produce nonsensical slicing, with no lower-bound or positivity guard.
page is never validated; page=0 or negative page flows unchecked into the offset computation, producing empty or wrong windows.
Logged 'page %d' reflects the raw 1-based page while the slice uses the buggy offset, so the log line misrepresents which records were actually returned.
Every method hand-rolls connect + try/finally instead of using a context manager / connection reuse, duplicating boilerplate and risking leaks on future edits.
